content.text: An alert without a runbook link is an incident waiting to go wrong. When a page fires at 3am, the on-call engineer should be able to click a single URL in the notification and immediately see: what the alert means, what to check first, which PromQL queries diagnose it, and who to escalate to. The alternative — searching through wikis and Slack history under time pressure — wastes critical minutes and increases error risk.

This document covers the linking standard (what the alert annotation must contain), the runbook format (what each page must include), infrastructure for validating links are not broken, and a complete example runbook for a Claude latency alert.
Every alert rule must carry a
annotation with a full HTTPS URL. The URL must:
Resolve to an existing, non-redirecting page (validated in CI).
Point to a page specific to this alert — not a generic observability wiki home page.
Remain stable — changing the URL breaks every existing alert notification that was bookmarked.

# Correct: specific runbook URL in every alert
- alert: ClaudeLatencyWarning
  expr: |
    histogram_quantile(0.99, ...) > 5
  for: 5m
  labels:
    severity: warning
  annotations:
    summary: "Claude p99 latency elevated for {{ $labels.feature }}"
    description: "p99 is {{ $value | humanizeDuration }}. Threshold is 5s."
    runbook: https://wiki.example.com/runbooks/claude-latencywarning
    dashboard: https://grafana.example.com/d/claude-golden-signals?var-feature={{ $labels.feature }}

# Wrong: no runbook, generic description
- alert: ClaudeLatencyWarning
  expr: |
    histogram_quantile(0.99, ...) > 5
  annotations:
    summary: "Latency high"
variable in the dashboard URL so the link opens directly to the affected feature's data, not the full multi-feature view.
Every runbook page must follow this six-section structure. Sections that are missing or incomplete make the runbook useless under incident pressure:
Plain-English explanation of what condition triggered the alert and why it matters
Numbered list of things to verify first, without any commands
PromQL, kubectl, or CLI commands that reveal the root cause
Ordered list of actions to stop user impact; include rollback instructions
Who to contact next if the on-call engineer cannot resolve
Team name, Slack channel, PagerDuty escalation policy
Links to related alerts and the relevant Grafana panels
The following is a full runbook for
that meets the format standard. Use it as a template.

# Runbook: ClaudeLatencyWarning / ClaudeLatencyPage

**Alert:** `ClaudeLatencyWarning` (p99 > 5s) / `ClaudeLatencyPage` (p99 > 30s)
**Owner:** AI Platform Team — [#ai-platform-oncall](slack://...)
**Dashboard:** [Claude Golden Signals](https://grafana.example.com/d/claude-golden-signals)

---

## What This Alert Means

The 99th-percentile response latency for Claude API calls on the affected feature
has exceeded the SLO threshold for at least 5 minutes. Most requests are still
completing, but the slowest 1% of users are experiencing significantly degraded
response times or timeouts.

## Immediate Checks (< 5 minutes)

1. Open the [Claude Golden Signals dashboard](https://grafana.example.com/d/claude-golden-signals)
   and set the `feature` variable to the affected feature from the alert label.
2. Check whether p50 latency is also elevated (if yes: widespread, not tail-only).
3. Check the error rate panel — is there a correlated spike in `rate_limit` errors?
4. Check [status.anthropic.com](https://status.anthropic.com) for an upstream incident.
5. Check whether a deployment happened in the last 30 minutes: `git log --oneline main -10`.

## Diagnostic Queries

**Current p99 per feature:**
```promql
histogram_quantile(0.99,
  sum by (feature, le)(rate(claude_api_request_duration_seconds_bucket[5m]))
)
```

**Is it limited to one feature or all features?**
```promql
histogram_quantile(0.99,
  sum by (feature, le)(rate(claude_api_request_duration_seconds_bucket[5m]))
)
```
If all features are elevated simultaneously, this is likely an upstream Anthropic issue.

**Are long prompts driving it?**
```promql
histogram_quantile(0.99,
  sum by (le)(rate(claude_prompt_tokens_bucket[5m]))
)
```
If prompt tokens are unusually high, a recent prompt change may be the cause.

**Circuit breaker state:**
```promql
claude_circuit_breaker_state{feature="<affected-feature>"}
```
If 1 (OPEN), requests are being rejected — the breaker opened due to prior errors.

## Mitigation Steps

1. **If upstream incident (Anthropic is down):** Enable fallback mode (returns cached
   or degraded responses). Notify affected users via status page.
2. **If circuit breaker is OPEN:** Wait for auto-recovery (HALF_OPEN after 60s).
   Do not manually reset unless you understand why it opened.
3. **If prompt tokens are high:** Identify which prompt template changed recently.
   Revert the change with `git revert <commit>` and deploy.
4. **If rate limiting:** Reduce concurrent requests — lower worker concurrency in
   `config/claude.yaml` from current value to 50% and redeploy.
5. **If no obvious cause:** Enable debug logging with
   `kubectl set env deployment/claude-proxy LOG_LEVEL=debug -n production`
   and inspect slow-request traces in Jaeger.

## Escalation

- If latency > 60s or duration > 20 min: escalate to **AI Platform lead** (PagerDuty
  escalation policy `claude-p1`).
- If upstream Anthropic issue confirmed: open a support ticket at
  https://support.anthropic.com and notify `#engineering-leadership`.

## Related Alerts and Dashboards

- [`ClaudeErrorRateWarning`](https://wiki.example.com/runbooks/claude-errorrate) — often
  co-fires with latency issues
- [`ClaudeCircuitBreakerOpen`](https://wiki.example.com/runbooks/claude-circuitbreaker)
- [Claude Cost Overview dashboard](https://grafana.example.com/d/claude-cost-overview)
  (check for unusual token burn during the incident)
Use a predictable, machine-generatable URL pattern. An on-call engineer should be able to guess the runbook URL from the alert name:
Warning and page versions of the same symptom share a single runbook — the diagnosis and mitigations are the same; only the urgency differs. Link both alert names to the same URL.
Broken runbook links are the most common form of alert debt. Validate them in CI every time alert YAML changes:

# .github/workflows/alerts.yaml — add runbook validation step
      - name: Validate runbook links
        run: python3 scripts/validate_runbook_links.py
Alertmanager can embed Grafana URLs directly in PagerDuty and Slack notifications using Go template syntax in the
section. Use Grafana's variable syntax to pass the alerting label values:

This generates a URL that opens the Golden Signals dashboard pre-filtered to the exact feature from the alert, over the last hour from when the alert fired. The engineer sees the relevant data immediately, without having to set variables manually.
For PagerDuty specifically, configure the Alertmanager PagerDuty receiver to include the runbook and dashboard links in the incident details:

# alertmanager.yml — PagerDuty receiver with links in details
receivers:
  - name: pagerduty
    pagerduty_configs:
      - service_key: $PAGERDUTY_SERVICE_KEY
        details:
          runbook: '{{ index .Alerts 0 | .Annotations.runbook }}'
          dashboard: '{{ index .Alerts 0 | .Annotations.dashboard }}'
          description: '{{ index .Alerts 0 | .Annotations.description }}'
        links:
          - href: '{{ index .Alerts 0 | .Annotations.runbook }}'
            text: 'Runbook'
          - href: '{{ index .Alerts 0 | .Annotations.dashboard }}'
            text: 'Dashboard'
A runbook that was accurate at launch becomes misleading after six months. Build runbook maintenance into the same quarterly process as dashboard ownership review:
field to every runbook page header.
During quarterly review, an engineer must execute the diagnostic queries in a staging environment to confirm they still work.
When a metric is renamed or a command changes, update the runbook in the same PR that changes the metric.
After any significant incident, update the runbook with new diagnostic steps discovered during the incident (within 24 hours, as part of the post-incident action items).
Track stale runbooks with the ownership audit script — add a check for
last_reviewed < 6 months; links valid; queries tested
Schedule review in next quarterly cycle
Fix or create immediately before merging alert change
Alert it references no longer exists
Archive runbook; remove from CI validation

The best time to update a runbook is immediately after using it during an incident, while the steps that were wrong (or missing) are fresh in memory. Add "update runbook" as a standard action item in every post-incident review template.
Alert-to-runbook linking is the connective tissue between an alert firing and an engineer knowing what to do. The standard is simple: every alert annotation carries a
URL pre-filtered to the affected label values, and a description with the current metric value. Runbooks follow a six-section format and are maintained alongside the alert definitions they support.
Every alert annotation must include a `runbook` URL — CI should fail on missing or broken links.
Warning and page variants of the same symptom share one runbook page.
Runbook URLs follow a predictable convention: `https://wiki/runbooks/claude-<alertname-lowercase>`.
Dashboard links in annotations should include label variables so the link opens pre-filtered.
Update runbooks within 24 hours of any incident that surfaced gaps or inaccuracies.
